coredata.dc:title: Transformation of crystalline starch nanoparticles into highly luminescent carbon nanodots: Toxicity studies and their applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sonthanasamy, Regina Sisika A.
Ahmad, Wan Yaacob Wan
Fazry, Shazrul
Hassan, Nurul I.
Lazim, Azwan Mat
coredata.dc:description: Being abundant in many tropical part of the world, Dioscorea sp. as food is limited due to its toxicity. However polysaccharides derive from these tubers could be important for other applications. Here we developed a Highly Luminescent Carbon Nanodots (C-dots) via acid hydrolysis of Gadong starch (GS). The hydrolysis rate of GS increased from 49% to 86% within 7 days while the X-ray diffraction showed the native GS particle is a C-crystalline type. The GS particles were either round or oval with diameters ranging from 50–90nm. Further acid dehydration and surface oxidation reduced the size of GS nanoparticles to 6–25nm. The C-dots produced a fluorescent emission at wavelength 441nm. Toxicity tests demonstrate that zebrafish embryo were able to tolerate the C-dots for 48h after exposure. This study has successfully demonstrated a novel approach of converting GS into excellent fluorescent C-dot.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dioscorea hispida
Acid hydrolysis
Nanocrystal
Starch
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Starch nanocrystals from D. hispida species
Fluorescent carbon nanodots from D. hispida starch nanocrystals

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported, in part, by research grants DLP-2014-019 , FRGS/1/2014/ST01/UKM/03/1 , GUP-2014- 079 and DIP-2014-016 given by Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia and the Ministry of Education .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Morphology of native Dioscorea hispida starch under FE-SEM (b) Morphology of acid hydrolysed Dioscorea hispida starch under FE-SEM (c) TEM image of Gadong starch nanoparticle.
X-ray diffraction pattern of Native and hydrolysed Gadong starch.
TEM image of C-dots from Gadong starch nanoparticles.
X-ray diffraction pattern of C-dots from Gadong starch nanoparticles.

Fluorescent emission spectra of C-dots recorded specifically with excitation at 290nm (purple), 320nm (blue) and 260nm (oren) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).
Toxicity test of carbon nanodots with Embryo of Zebra fish.
Fluorescence enhancement by UV wavelength at 365nm. (a) View without UV-lamp (b) view with UV lamp.

row.entry: Aromatic CH stretch (Usually from polysaccharides, especially from α-glucose)

head.ce:title: Transformation of crystalline starch nanoparticles into highly luminescent carbon nanodots: Toxicity studies and their applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences and Food Technology, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi, 43600 Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Sciences and Food Technology, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Bioscience and Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi, 43600 Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Bioscience and Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Being abundant in many tropical part of the world, Dioscorea sp. as food is limited due to its toxicity. However polysaccharides derive from these tubers could be important for other applications. Here we developed a Highly Luminescent Carbon Nanodots (C-dots) via acid hydrolysis of Gadong starch (GS). The hydrolysis rate of GS increased from 49% to 86% within 7 days while the X-ray diffraction showed the native GS particle is a C-crystalline type. The GS particles were either round or oval with diameters ranging from 50–90nm. Further acid dehydration and surface oxidation reduced the size of GS nanoparticles to 6–25nm. The C-dots produced a fluorescent emission at wavelength 441nm. Toxicity tests demonstrate that zebrafish embryo were able to tolerate the C-dots for 48h after exposure. This study has successfully demonstrated a novel approach of converting GS into excellent fluorescent C-dot.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) were known as a substitution candidate for the conventional phosphor materials (Hong et al., 2012; Li et al., 2011a; Li et al., 2010a; Lightcap & Kamat, 2012; Song & Yang, 2012; Zhao et al., 2012; Zhuo, Shao, & Lee, 2012) and widely used in various filed such as biomedical and bioimaging due to their unique optical, electronic and fluorescent properties (Zhai et al., 2012). However interest in QDs was reduced for being costly, ineffective, complicated and impractical in real applications (Li, Kang, Liu, & Lee, 2012; Uu, Li, Zhan, Liu, & Huang, 2011). The usage of QDs is slowly declining due to the introduction of carbon based quantum dots. The explosion of nanotechnology in 2004 proposed new types of carbon nanodots via the electrophoresis of carbon nanotube thereby attracting researchers to better study carbon nanodots to replace QDs (Xu et al., 2004). Recently, carbon based quantum dots known as carbon nanodots (C-dots) have drawn considerable interest due to their stable photoluminescence property, less toxicity, high biocompatibility, cost effectiveness, good fluorescent properties, easy-functionalisation, excellent water solubility, high sensitivity, excellent selectivity to target analyte, tunable fluorescence emission and excitation and high quantum yield (Baker & Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012; Saxena & Sarkar, 2012;). However, understanding the origin of fluorescence in C-dots is far from sufficient and information regarding the nanostructure and surface of C-dots remain unclear.

Based on the impressive advances of C-dots, various methods have been established to synthesis non-toxic fluorescing C-dots which is categorised into top-down and bottom-up methods (Cao et al., 2007; Chin, Yazi, Pang, & Ng, 2012; Jia, Li, & Wang, 2012; Li et al., 2010b; Li et al., 2011a; Li et al., 2011b; Liu, Ye, & Mao, 2007; Liu, Tian, Wang, Luo, & Sun, 2012; Liang, Ma, Shi, Li, & Yang, 2013a; Long et al., 2012; Ma, Ming, Huang, Liu, & Kang, 2012; Ming et al., 2012; Qin, Lu, Asiri, Al-Youbi, & Sun, 2013; Sahu, Behera, Maiti, & Mohapatra, 2012; Wang, Wang, & Chen, 2012; Wu, Li, Tan, Wu, & Liu, 2015). Different starting materials have been used in the preparation of C-dots (Das, Liu, Yeom, Kim, & Richards, 2014; Dong et al., 2012a; Hsu, Shih, Lee, & Chang, 2012; Lai, Hsiao, Peng, & Chou, 2012; Liang, Ma, Shi, Li, & Yang, 2013b; Liu et al., 2012; Shi et al., 2015a; Wang et al., 2011; Wu et al., 2015; Zhu, Zhai, & Dong, 2012). Nevertheless, the majority of the synthesis methods require complicated equipment, catalyst, non-environmental friendly chemicals or harsh experimental condition which affect the production cost (Tan, Romainor, Chin, & Ng, 2014). In advance, C-dots synthesised from different methods and various sources were applicable in numerous biological and chemical fields (Ahmed, Laino, Calzon, & Garcia, 2015; Guo, Wang, Shao, & Jiang, 2013; Liu, Zhao, & Zhang, 2014; Qin et al., 2013; Qu, Chen, Zheng, Cao, & Liu, 2013; Quyang, 2013; Shi et al., 2015a; Shi et al., 2015b; Wang et al., 2015; Xu, Lai, Feng, Weng, & Huang, 2014; Yazid, Chin, Pang, & Ng, 2013). For example, C-dots based on carbon powder with diameter size 4–5nm was synthesised using laser radiation while C-dots with size 1nm in size were synthesised from candle soot by combustion and refluxed with nitric acid for 12h (Ray, Saha, Jana, & Sarkar, 2009). These synthesised fluorescence particles had been used in fluorescent-based cell imaging applications. Starch based fluorescent C-dots, however, are poorly studied compared to other carbon-based materials such as carbon nanotube, citric acid, and glycerol (Chin et al., 2012).

Nanocrystals from polymeric material can be utilised to synthesise C-dots. Polymeric nanocrystals can be extracted from naturally occurring polysaccharides such as starch, cellulose, and chitin due to their semi-crystalline nature. In recent years, the demand for starch nanocrystals (SNCs) has seen an increase in various sectors. For example, these non-toxic nanoparticles have been approved as a suitable candidate for a renewable nano-filler (Chin, Azman, & Peng, 2014a; LeCorre, Bras, & Dufresne, 2010). Moreover, SNCs have also been proposed as an alternative material to replace carbon black used to reinforce natural rubber. SNCs are also known as an excellent material to be used for drug delivery systems and have been employed as a slow-release drug-carrier (Han, Borjihan, Bai, Chen, & Jing, 2008; Rodrigues & Emeja, 2012; Simi & Abraham, 2007; Yu, Xiao, Tong, Chen, & Liu, 2007). SNCs are also used in biotechnology as a nano-encapsulation agent or as a stabilising agent for nano-emulsions (Chin, Yazid, & Pang, 2014b; Tzoumaki, Moschakis, Kiosseoglou, & Biliaderis, 2011;). In biomedical applications, SNCs have been introduced into polymeric matrices in order to improve their mechanical strength and biodegradability (Kristo & Biliaderis, 2007).

Dioscorea hispida Dennst. is categorised as a Lasiophyton and taxonomically ascribed to the Dioscoreacea family and Dioscorea genus (Baah, 2009). There are 1137 species of Dioscorea around the world, but only 600 species are reported to be harmless (Agbar-Egbe & Treche, 1995; Nashriyah, Yusoff, Tajuddin, Ngah, & Rejab, 2010; Poornima & Rai, 2009). The Gadong tuber is recognised as a largely abandoned species of Dioscorea due to containing poisonous alkaloids known as dioscorine (Ashri et al., 2014; Azman et al., 2015; Hudzari, 2012). To obtain C-dots, various starting materials were used and passivated with various compound such as BPEI and PEG1500N or combining several chemicals to enhance fluorescent properties (Cao et al., 2007; Dong et al., 2012b; Sun et al., 2006; Sun et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2013). On the other hand, no literature reported on the preparation of C-dots based on the exotic wild yam, D. hispida. Apart from that, none demonstrated that starch based C-dots can act as a fluorescent ink.

This research explores the synthesis of C-dots by a simple synthesis route from a low cost precursor, Gadong starch and we investigate its resistance to toxicity and fluorescent properties. The SNCs and C-dots were then characterised to determine its optimum performance. The prepared nanocrystals and synthesised C-dots have been characterised by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to investigate the morphology, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transformed infrared FTIR, UV–vis, and fluorescence measurements to introduce this C-dots as a new type of fluorescent ink. Further investigations were carried out to determine the toxicity level of the synthesised C-dots. The C-dots were tested on 1-h post fertilization zebra fish embryos.

All chemicals were of reagent grade and were used without further purification. Gadong tuber was obtained from Terengganu, Malaysia. Native Gadong starch was extracted from Gadong tuber according to the procedure reported by Nand, Charan, Rohindra, and Khurma (2008). Sulphuric acid (95–97%) was purchased from Friendemann Schmidt (USA), Ultrapure water (∼18.2MΩ, 25°C) was obtained from Water Purify System (Sartorius, Model arium®611DI). Sodium hydroxides were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA).

Embryos were obtained from the Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biotechnology and Biomolecular Sciences, Universiti Putra Malaysia. E3 embryo media (5.03mM NaCl, 0.17mM KCl, 0.33mM CaCl2·2H2O, 0.33mM MgSO4·2H2O and 0.1% (w/v) methylene blue) were used to wash and maintain viable fertilised eggs at room temperature at all times.

Acid hydrolysis was preformed according to the method reported by Kim, Lee, Kim, Lim, and Lim (2012). Native Gadong starch (15g, dry) was dispersed in an aqueous sulphuric acid solution (100mL, 3.16M), and the dispersion was stirred for 7 days at 40°C. On the seventh day, the dispersion was neutralised by washing with distilled water filtered by centrifuging the suspension at 20,000rpm for 20min. The wet precipitate was then washed with distilled water and stirred for 30min before the dispersion was again centrifuged at 20,000rpm for 20min. This washing process was repeated several times to remove the residual sulphate. The precipitated starch hydrolysate was then freeze-dried, and the solid weight of the starch hydrolysate was measured. The degree of hydrolysis (%) was calculated as the percent mass ratio of the dissolved starch solids to the initial starch solids.
(1)
Degree
of
hydrolysis
( % ) =
Dissolved
starch
solids
Initial
starch
solids
× 100

Starch nanoparticles were prepared from Gadong tuber based on Kim's et al. (Kim et al., 2012) method. The starch nanoparticles were converted to carbon nanoparticles based on Chin's et al. (Chin et al., 2012) method by dehydration with concentrated sulphuric acid. In typical synthesis, 2.0g of starch nanoparticles were dispersed in 5mL of ultrapure water and 8.0mL of concentrated sulphuric acid was added to the starch solution. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 40min, followed by the addition of 40mL of ultra-pure water. The black carbon nanoparticles produced was collected by centrifugation (20,000g, 20min) and washed three times with ultra-pure water.

The black carbon nanoparticles were dispersed in 20mL of nitric acid (2.0M) and the mixture was refluxed for 1h. The solution was neutralised by sodium hydroxide at room temperature (Chin et al., 2012).

Morphology and particle size of the starch nanoparticles and C-dots were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Philips) operated at 300kV. The precipitates (10mg, dry basis) were dispersed in 5mL of water and then ultrasonically homogenised for 3min at 13,000rpm to ensure dispersion of the starch particles. A drop of the suspension was deposited on a carbon-coated microscopy grid. The grid was negatively stained with a drop of uranyl acetate solution (2% w/v) before it was dried at room temperature (Kim et al., 2012). Besides that, Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) was used to confirm the shape and size of the starch nanoparticles. The liquid C-dots were diluted with ultra-pure water in ratio of 1:5 (mL). A drop of the suspension was deposited on a carbon-coated microscopy grid and dried at room temperature (Chin et al., 2012). Twenty particles from both the nanoparticles and C-dots particles were randomly selected and then their sizes were measured using iTEM software.

The particle size distribution of the native and hydrolysed Gadong starch was determined using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) (Malver Instruments Ltd.). Deionized water starch dispersions with concentration of 0.01mg/mL were prepared using a tip sonicator during 20min. DLS measurements were immediately performed in order to avoid sample precipitation (Lamanna, Morales, Garcia, & Goyanes, 2013). The experiment was carried out three times to validate the data.

X-ray diffractogram of native, hydrolysed Gadong starch and C-dots were obtained using a diffractometer (Bruker/A8 Advance) with CuK radiation (Ni filter) at a target voltage, 40kV and current, 40mA respectively. The scanning range and rate were 3°–40° (2Ø) and 0.025°/0.1s, respectively (Chin et al., 2012; Kim et al., 2012).

The crystallinity index of native and hydrolysed Gadong starch was used for quantifying crystallinity changes as functions of the hydrolysis time. The crystallinity index, CIHW, was computed with the classical method by Hermans and Weidinger (Hermans & Weidinger, 1948). Briefly, the diffractograms were deconvoluted using Ave software and the starch crystallinity index, CIHW, was calculated by the following equation (OECD, 2013):
(2)
CI
HW
=
A r
−
A a
A r
x
100
where, A a is the value of the area under the curve corresponding to the amorphous portion of the diffractrograms and A r is the total area of the diffractograms.

UV absorbance of the sample was determined using UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2450 SHIMADZU). The suspension was diluted with ultra-pure water in ratio of 1:10 (mL) and poured into a cuvette. Ultra-pure water was used as reference. The absorbance of sample was monitored between 250nm and 500nm and made in triplicated (Chin et al., 2012).

Fluorescence of C-dots was analysed by spectrofluorometer (Perkin Elmer LS 55- Luminescence Spectrophotometer). The suspension was diluted with ultra-pure water in the ratio of 1:10 (mL) and poured into a cuvette. The fluorescent spectrum of C-dots was recorded by a comprehensive scan with excitation ranging from 200 to 400nm and emission was recorded from 300 to 600nm (Chin et al., 2012).
FTIR was used to characterise the organic functional groups on C-dots. The liquefied C-dots with sodium chloride pellet were used and the spectra were measured ranging from 400 to 4000cm−1.

In a 96-well plate, the embryos were separated in a single well 1 hour post fertilisation (hpf) and they were maintained in 180μL of E3 embryo media at room temperature. About 20μL of 0.5mg/mL C-dots were added into each well (n =3). Using light microscope at 4× magnification, the embryos were observed at 24 and 48 hpf. This procedure of the FET tests were carried out in accordance to the OECD draft guideline (OECD, 2006, 2013).
This section will discuss both starch nanocrystal and carbon nanodots from D. hispida species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Starch nanocrystals from D. hispida species

ce:section.ce:para: The hydrolysis profile of the D. hispida (Gadong) starch during the 7 day period is shown in the supporting information (SI) section (Fig. S1). The two stages of the hydrolysis trend that can be observed are a fast hydrolysis in the early stage (0–5 days) followed by a slow hydrolysis stage (between 5 and 7 days). The degree of hydrolysis was increased rapidly in first stage from 49% to 79% followed by slow increment to 86% on the seventh day.

Since starch granules are made up of nano-sized blocklets and acid hydrolysis was conducted to isolate nano-sized particles from microsized starch granules. Hydrolysis is a “releasing process” of the nano-sized blocklets from microparticles (Kim, Park, Kim, & Lim, 2013; LeCorre, Bras, & Dufresne, 2011). The releasing of nano-sized particles are slow on the later stage compared to the initial stage of hydrolysis. The hydrolysis rate is indirectly proportional to the yield of nano starch. This is because the starch had been solubilised by acid during the treatment and starch was hydrolysed more rapidly in the initial stage.

In general, all starch granules had channels and pores connecting the internal to the external environment. Therefore, the hydrolytic acid had access to the interior of the granules via channels resulting in its high digestibility. Hence, this report hypothesised that the hydrolytic acid diffused into the starch granules started to react on the amorphous region by attacking the hydrogen ion and this causes fast hydrolysis over 5 days. After the 5th day, slow hydrolysis is presumably due to the degradation of crystalline region and the rearrangement of the amylopectin chain (Kim et al., 2012; LeCorre et al., 2011). The slow hydrolysis trend is also due to the large degree of polymerization (DP) of amylose which might slow down the hydrolysing action. Wang and Wang (2001) stated that a larger DP required more time for hydrolysing agent to degrade the chain length in starch molecules.

In addition, although the hydrolysis directly influenced the acid type, acid concentration, temperature and time, it is also dependant on the botanical source and different levels of structure/chain length of amylose and amylopectin.

Native and hydrolysed Gadong starch was observed under Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1 . Based on the FE-SEM image, native starch granules exhibited a regular, polygonal shape with smooth surface. The hydrolysed starch granules shows a round shape with less smooth surface accounting for different fragments of the granules that has been generated by acid hydrolysis.

In principle, the number of SNCs are increased at longer periods of acid hydrolysis reaction. Kim et al. (2012) showed that nanocrystals can be obtained after 5–7 days of acid hydrolysis when the amorphous lamellae of starches were eroded and nanocrystalline components could be released from growth rings. By extending the hydrolysis period (5 days), few changes have been observed such as shape, size, and surface smoothness. In general, after the first day of hydrolysis, only some starch granule presented slight exo-erosion on the surface, gradually increasing by the third day but with most of the starch granules maintaining their polygonal shape. Due to extension made (more than 5 days), deformation on the surface of acid-treated granules can clearly be observed. Adhesion between some of the granules and progressive surface erosion and fracture is favoured by strongly stirring the granule morphology (Coello et al., 2014; LeCorre et al., 2011).

The fragmentation mostly resulted from the cleavage at the amorphous regions instead of crystallines regions, but little changes in the amylopectin branch chain length occurred. This was probably due to the variant procedures applied for isolation or morphology analysis. The microstructure of starch granules was strongly disrupted by the action of hydrogen ion, depending on acid concentration and temperature. The size, shape and crystallinity obtained can differ according to the starch origin and manufacturing process.

It is noteworthy that the hydrolysed starch particles exist as a combination of nano and micro particles depending on the hydrolysis duration. During the hydrolysis process, SNCs are continuously produced as starch granules are fragmented by acid, but resulting SNCs may tend to form aggregates that readily sediment as microparticles. The existence of nanocrystal aggregates could suggest the presence of a high number of OH-group per individual nanocrystal, which is strongly associated by hydrogen bonding (Lamanna et al., 2013). Liu, Wu, Chen, and Chang (2009) suggested that starch nanocrystals tend to form micro-scale aggregate through van der Waals force and hydrogen bond, which could readily sediment as precipitates.

Particle size distribution was used to evaluate the effects of hydrolysis on starch granules, as presented in the SI section. Native starch showed a long normal particle size distribution, which is on the right side. Under the acid action, the span of distribution broadens with the peak shifting increasing to the left as hydrolysis time proceeds, thereby decreasing the mean and size diameter of the particles. The size of the particles are 4.0±1.1μm as shown in Fig. S2(a). For SNCs, the particles size are between 50–90nm and 105–295nm with a mean of 127.7±24.5nm (Fig. S2(b)).

The resulting granules have a quite round/oval shape where the original polygonal geometry is no longer found. The mean particles size declines when the hydrolysis period was extended (more than 5–7 days). The size of native starch particles and nanoparticles was non-uniform resulting in two peaks due to the aggregation of starch nanoparticles.

XRD patterns of native and hydrolysed Gadong starch are shown in Fig. 2 . The dotted vertical lines were used for highlighting three prominent intensity peaks at about 15°, 17° and 22° diffraction degree, which are indicative of A-type crystallinities. Our result is in agreement with those of Millan-Testa, Mendez-Montealvo, Ottenhof, Farhat, and Bello-Perez (2005), reporting the same three prominent peaks described in Fig. 5(a). Additionally, a small peak at 5.6° diffraction degrees indicative the presence of B-type crystal was also displayed from native and hydrolysed starch. Therefore, Gadong starch is categorised as C-type crystallinity.

The Gadong starch sample displayed a C-type pattern, which represents a combination of A and B-type polymorphs. The hydrolysed starch showed no significant change in diffraction pattern when compared to native starch. The peaks at 17° was much more prominent than the other peaks and the peak at 23° was border. The relatively weak peak at 20.0° (2θ) in Gadong starch indicated that crystalline AM-lipid complexes were removed upon acid hydrolysis (Kim et al., 2012).

The native Gadong starch showed a typical C-type pattern with a 41% of crystallinity and this was increased to 51% of crystallinity for hydrolysed Gadong. If the hydrolysis occurred exclusively on the amorphous region, the residual hydrolysate should have pure crystalline region of the starch (2 days). However, the crystallinity increment observed by the XRD analysis was relatively minor and thus, the hydrolysis during the 2 days of hydrolysis was little selective of the amorphous region. The acid hydrolysis may increase the degree of crystallinity because it was assumed to preferentially cleave the starch chains residing in the amorphous regions. Finally, the increase in crystallinity not only removed the amorphous phase but also affected the rearrangement of the amylopectin chain.

Simultaneously, rearrangements of the chain segments to a more ordered arrangement (annealing) may also occur given that the mobility of the starch chains was enhanced by hydrolysis. Although crystalline lamellae are more resistant to hydrolysis by chemicals than the amorphous fraction, sulphuric acid may disrupt the crystalline structure after the depletion of the rings in the amorphous region. This finding corroborates with previous facts establishing that, in the first stage, acid hydrolysis predominantly affected the amorphous regions so that residual starch had more refined crystallinity. Kim et al. (2012) reported that the amorphous erosion occurred in the early stage of acid hydrolysis because of its physical accessibility and the disruption of crystalline regions could follow in the late stage. Although the amorphous erosion was evident by the XRD hydrolysis, the disruption of crystalline arrangement occurred concurrently during this period. Crystalline patterns of all starches were not markedly changed upon the acid hydrolysis for 5 days, which was in agreement with a previous study, although diffraction intensity was somewhat changed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent carbon nanodots from D. hispida starch nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: Transformation of starch nanocrystal into fluorescent carbon nanodots were obtained by combustion and reflux process.

TEM image of the prepared SNCs from Gadong starch were carbonized into C-dots and shown in Fig. 3 . The synthesised C-dots from starch precursors have a size diameter ranging from 6 to 25nm. In comparison, the size of C-dot synthesised in this research was smaller than those C-dots from sago starch prepared by Chin et al. (2012). As observed in the TEM images, the dehydration and surface oxidation process has changed the morphology of the starch precursors. The size varied from 16–35nm to 6–25nm whereas the shape of the starch precursors were retarder. According to Chin et al. (2012), there is a high possibility that such a phenomenon occurred based on the different precursors of starch nanoparticles. This research has successfully proved that different starch precursors gave various size C-dots.

Besides that, the carbon nanoparticles synthesised from SNCs is black in colour and completely insoluble in water as they are hydrophobic in nature. When this black carbon nanoparticle was refluxed with nitric acid, light brownish yellow coloured supernatant solution was obtained. Brownish yellow supernatant indicates a part of carbon particles become small and water-soluble during the refluxing process. C-dots become highly water soluble due to its hydrophilic nature. The top right red box in Fig. 3 shows the aggregation of C-dots in 22,000×. A similar type of aggregation was observed by Ray et al. (2009).

Transformation of starch nanoparticles into C-dots were analysed using X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD). Fig. 4 shows the X-ray diffraction pattern of C-dots. The absence of any distinctive diffraction peak indicated that the sample was mostly amorphous in nature. This similar phenomenon has been previously reported by Chin et al. (2012).

The UV absorbance spectrum of C-dots sample was displayed in Fig. 5 . C-dots exhibit an absorbance peak with mean of 1.796±0.00476 at 290nm. The similar trend was reported by Peng and Sejdic (2009) with UV absorbance peak at 290nm and Chin et al. (2012) with UV absorbance peak at 299nm.

This result shows that the particles synthesis in this study was smaller than the particles synthesised by Chin et al. (2012). The absorption in C-dots is typically ascribed to the presence of n→π* electron transition associated with CO functional group respectively.

The C-dots was recorded to emit fluorescence with broadband having peak at 441nm. A comprehensive scan with excitation ranging from 200 to 400nm (interval of 10nm) was performed and the fluorescence emitted was recorded from 300 to 600nm respectively. Emission of C-dots was recorded and the peak was shown in Fig. 6 . Although various excitations tested with the same emission wavelength, the fluorescence peak at 441nm remains the same. Therefore, this research hypothesises that the emission was independent to the excitation. This phenomenon was previously reported elsewhere (Chin et al., 2012).

From the results, the multi-emission profile of the synthesised C-dots is due to the narrow distribution for parameters such as size, surface defect, band gap, and surface structure (Chin et al., 2012). C-dots synthesis from Gadong starch has luminescent properties which is clearly visible in the emission spectrum.

Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy provided information about the bonding configuration of the C-dots and the location of the hydrogen atom within the particles. The FTIR spectrum obtained for C-dots synthesised from native Gadong starch nanoparticles are shown in Fig. S3. Table 1 shows the summary of the results obtained for C-dots.

The extinction spectra in the 400–4000cm−1 range showed broad continuum absorption with several superimposed bands while the majority of the bands correspond to the vibrational features of the different functional group of chromophores attached to the internal structure of the C-dots. The main differences observed in the band profile were related to the nature of the precursor. Most of the distinctive bands were attributed to CH and CC bonds at positions typical for hydrogenated carbon compound (Galvez, Boime, Reynaud, Clinard, & Rouzaud, 2002).

The main bands can be divided into four groups. The first group is in the range 3500–3000cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching vibration. Peak at 3433cm−1 assigned to the OH stretching vibration (Ray et al., 2009; Yan et al., 2014). The second group band between 1500 and 2000cm−1 is due to the carbonyl stretching. The peak at 1682cm−1 assigned to the CO stretching vibration means that some carbonyl compounds are present in these sample (Ray et al., 2009; Wu et al., 2015). The hydroxyl peak at 3433cm−1 assigned to the absorption of carbonyl become medium strong. The third group of bond is found between 500 and1000cm−1 characteristic of polysaccharide sample. Those characters of peaks intensities and position 2930, 1130, 1104, 997, 925 and 832cm−1 in the IR spectrum display the characteristic absorption of polysaccharides especially the peak at 832cm−1 belong to the characteristic peak of α-glucose (Prasad, Kumar, & Sharanappa, 2011). This was proven where there is a peak at 829cm−1 indicates aromatic CH stretch and the precursor used in this experiment was polysaccharides. Finally, the additional peak can be seen at 2092cm−1 which corresponding to the CH stretch (Ray et al., 2009) and 1456cm−1 for aliphatic CH3 bond.

Fig. 7 shows the C-dots were introduced into fish embryo to evaluate their toxicity capabilities using confocal microscopy test in vitro. Cell viability studies suggested that C-dots exhibited cytotoxicity and posed no significant toxic effect (Fig. S4). Within 48h, fish embryos exposed with the C-dots were observed to develop normally without any sign of lethality.

C-dots have been reported unable to infiltrate the cells’ nuclei which suggested that they did not disrupt genetic structure of the cell and caused toxicity (Jeong, Cho, Lim, Song, & Chung, 2009; Li et al., 2012). This supports our data that shows that our C-dots are not toxic and bio-compatible for biological uses.

The C-dots obtained from starch nanoparticles show strongest fluorescent emission at wavelength 441nm under various excitation wavelengths of 260nm, 290nm, and 320nm, as shown in Fig. 6. This result indicated this aqueous solution can be used as a new type of fluorescent ink or as a new type of biomarker. Therefore, the fluorescent properties of C-dots were further confirmed with UV-lamp. C-dots are invisible in daylight but it will be visible under the explosion of a UV-lamp. Fig. 8 proved that the synthesised C-dots from Gadong starch shows green fluorescent rays under the blue-light excitation at wavelength 365nm (Wang et al., 2014).

Based on the result, it was assumed that C-dots can be used as fluorescent ink to detect certain materials. Therefore, this research was fullerene with detecting natural and synthetic materials. Synthetic polyester and natural cotton are used in this research to ensure the fluorescent properties of C-dots on both the materials. Synthetic polyester material was immersed in diluted C-dots for 2min and both the synthetic materials with and without C-dots were viewed under daylight. When both materials viewed under UV-lamp with wavelength 365nm, it showed blue rays. From this result, it was predicted that C-dots would not be active or reactive on synthetic materials. This method was conducted on cotton where the cotton was immersed in 0.5mL of diluted C-dots for 2min and both the cotton, with and without C-dots, was viewed under the daylight. When this natural material was exposed to UV-lamp at 365nm, the cotton that contained C-dots showed green rays and the cotton without C-dots showed blue rays (Fig. S5). From this result, it was assumed that the C-dots were active on natural material. This phenomenon agreed with previous research on nylon and cotton threads (Qu, Wang, Lu, Liu, & Wang, 2012).

The reactivity of C-dots on biopolymer is still new to the nanotechnology field. Therefore, this research was supported by biopolymer material to ensure the reactivity of C-dots on biopolymer. Starch was used as a biopolymer material to determine the fluorescent character of C-dots. Hydrogel made from starch and without starch were examined by using C-dots. Both the hydrogels were soaked in diluted C-dots for 10min and viewed under daylight. Both hydrogels were continuously exposed to UV-lamp at 365nm and the hydrogel without starch showed blue rays whereas hydrogel with starch showed green rays under the blue ray excitation. From the result, it was assumed that the hydrogel which showed blue rays are made up with synthetic or artificial chemicals and does not contain any natural materials. The hydrogel which showed green rays indicates that natural material was present in the hydrogel (Fig. S6). Overall, the C-dots were biocompatible and could be used to distinguish natural from synthetic products.

This study has successfully demonstrated the transformation of SNCs into fluorescent C-dots from polysaccharides by an aqueous-based synthesis method. This method is simple and straightforward to synthesise C-dots compared to other methods such as electrochemical oxidation, pyrolysis, and others. In addition, this method reduced reaction time and the surface oxidation efficiently produces fluorescent C-dots in 1h with a size range between 6 and 25nm. This fluorescent C-dots is applicable for various fields such as biomedicine, agriculture, and others. This research introducing a synthesised C-dots from Gadong starch as a new type of fluorescent ink in detecting natural and synthetic materials. Additional study should focus on modification and toxicity for the biosensing and bioimaging utilisation. This research supports that the Gadong tuber is a viable alternative source of starch nanoparticles for future applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported, in part, by research grants DLP-2014-019, FRGS/1/2014/ST01/UKM/03/1, GUP-2014- 079 and DIP-2014-016 given by Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia and the Ministry of Education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.carbpol.2015.11.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:
